core_behavior.note: Model C explicitly states 404 must not be used for authorization failures and mandates uniform application across all endpoints, while Models A and B do not address these constraints; none of the models explicitly address the 403-vs-404 distinction for permission-denied scenarios in core behavior.

inputs.note: Model B lists request body and headers as explicit inputs, Model C adds path parameters and query parameters as distinct input types, while Model A describes only a generic resource identifier.

outputs.note: Model C mandates a specific nested JSON structure ({ error: { code, message } }), a Cache-Control: no-store header, message length limit, and string-only error code, while Models A and B use a flat structure and Model A allows string or numeric error code; none explicitly enforce error code as string-only per critical behaviors.

assumptions.note: Model C explicitly assumes content negotiation is not applied to error responses and that auth/authz is handled upstream, while Models A and B do not address these design choices.

failure_modes.note: Model C has an empty failure_modes array while Models A and B specify behaviors for internal errors and serialization failures.

edge_cases.note: Model B states an unsupported HTTP method on a non-existent resource returns 404, while Model A states it should return 405; none of the models address the 403-vs-404 permission scenario as an edge case per critical behaviors.

security_constraints.note: Model B adds a requirement for HTTPS/secure transport, Model C explicitly prohibits including the missing identifier value in the message, while Model A addresses neither; no model explicitly addresses not confirming existence of resources the caller is unauthorized to see.

performance_constraints.note: Model A specifies a 100ms threshold, Model B specifies a 500ms threshold, and Model C has an empty performance_constraints array.

summary: ASM (Assumption Divergence) was the most frequently assigned category, appearing in four of the eight fields.
